description:
You are a container-platform specialist who has just been handed three mock micro-service endpoints that live inside the training container.  
Your goal is to run a very small “smoke test” against each of the endpoints with curl and write a **single** log file that the CI system will later pick up.

Environment that already exists inside the container  
-----------------------------------------------------
1. Directory holding the mock services:  
   /home/user/mock_services  

2. Exact files and their expected content (each file **already exists**):  
   • /home/user/mock_services/auth.json  
     {"status":"ok","service":"auth"}⏎ (there is a single trailing newline)  
   • /home/user/mock_services/user.json  
     {"status":"ok","service":"user"}⏎  
   • /home/user/mock_services/payment.json  
     {"status":"ok","service":"payment"}⏎  

Because these are plain files, the “endpoint” URL you must pass to curl is the file-scheme URL, e.g.  
file:///home/user/mock_services/auth.json

Task you must accomplish  
------------------------
1. Use curl exactly once for every service (three times in total).  
2. For each call, gather BOTH  
   a) the HTTP response code (curl write-out token %{http_code}; for file:// this will be 000)  
   b) the number of **downloaded bytes** (curl write-out token %{size_download})  

3. Produce a single UTF-8 text file named /home/user/api_test.log.  
   • One logical line **per** service, ordered exactly as: auth, user, payment.  
   • Use the following strict, machine-checked format (no spaces):  
     <service_name>;<http_code>;<size_download>\n  
   • Example showing the required pattern (values below are the ones the CI will expect):  
     auth;000;33  
     user;000;33  
     payment;000;36  

   The file must end with a newline after the last line.

4. Do not create any extra files.  
5. Apart from normal curl progress (which you may suppress with -s), no additional console output is required; the existence and the exact contents of /home/user/api_test.log is what will be graded.

Hints (NOT mandatory)  
• The write-out option `-w` together with `-o /dev/null` and `-s` is an easy way to collect only the metadata you need.  
• You don’t need loops—three straightforward curl commands are enough for this exercise.

When done, /home/user/api_test.log must exactly match the example above byte for byte.
truth:
Pre-existing structure (already in place before the agent starts)
----------------------------------------------------------------
Directory: /home/user/mock_services (permissions 755, owner user)  

Files and their byte-accurate contents (with a trailing newline, 0x0a):  

1. /home/user/mock_services/auth.json (33 bytes)  
   {"status":"ok","service":"auth"}\n  

2. /home/user/mock_services/user.json (33 bytes)  
   {"status":"ok","service":"user"}\n  

3. /home/user/mock_services/payment.json (36 bytes)  
   {"status":"ok","service":"payment"}\n  

Expected artefact to be created by the agent
--------------------------------------------
File: /home/user/api_test.log (permissions 644, owner user)  
Exact content (including trailing newline):

auth;000;33\n
user;000;33\n
payment;000;36\n
name: task_000000_af82230d
